cpp_intro.title: C++ Overview
cpp_intro.summary: History, toolchain, standards (C++11/14/17/20), compilation model and basic workflow.
cpp_intro.details: C++ is a performance-oriented systems language suitable for low-level systems, games, and high-performance applications. Learn how the language evolved, the compilation model (preprocess → compile → link), and the typical developer toolchain (g++, clang, MSVC, CMake). Focus on modern idioms (RAII, smart pointers, range-based algorithms) rather than old C-style patterns.

examples.code:
#include <iostream>
int main(){ std::cout << "Hello C++" << std::endl; return 0; }

interview.q: What is C++ and how did it evolve?
interview.a: C++ is a general-purpose programming language that evolved from C by adding OOP, templates, and a richer standard library; it has evolved through standardized revisions (C++98,11,14,17,20,23).

interview.q: How does C++ compilation model work?
interview.a: C++ compilation is a multi-step process: preprocessing, compilation to object files, and linking of object files into an executable; headers provide declarations and .cpp files contain definitions.

interview.q: What are common toolchains for C++ development?
interview.a: Typical toolchains include GCC (g++), Clang/LLVM, and MSVC on Windows; CMake is commonly used for build orchestration.
-O2 enables optimization level 2 for speed; -g includes debug information for debuggers like gdb.
Undefined behavior is program behavior that the standard does not define, leading to unpredictable results; examples include out-of-bounds access and use-after-free.

interview.q: Why is RAII important in C++?
interview.a: RAII ties resource lifetime to object lifetime, ensuring resources are released in destructors, reducing leaks and improving exception safety.

interview.q: What are headers and include guards?
interview.a: Headers declare interfaces; include guards (#ifndef/#define) or #pragma once prevent double inclusion.
C++11 introduced move semantics, auto, lambda expressions, range-based for, smart pointers, and many other features.

interview.q: When to use C++ over higher-level languages?
interview.a: Use C++ for performance-critical systems, low-level control, systems programming, or when fine-grained resource control is required.

interview.q: What is the Standard Template Library (STL)?
interview.a: STL provides containers, algorithms, iterators, and utilities that enable generic programming with good performance.
A translation unit is a source file after preprocessing; each .cpp and included headers form a translation unit compiled into an object file.
Linker resolves symbol references between object files and libraries, producing an executable or library; unresolved symbols cause link errors.

interview.q: What are ABI and name mangling?
interview.a: ABI is the binary interface for function calling. C++ compilers use name mangling to encode function signatures; extern "C" disables mangling for C linkage.
Use package managers (vcpkg, Conan), submodules, or fetch content in CMake; prefer reproducible builds.

interview.q: What is inline and when to use it?
interview.a: inline suggests compiler to inline code and allows function definitions in headers without multiple-definition errors; modern compilers ignore explicit inline heuristics.

interview.q: What is the One Definition Rule (ODR)?
interview.a: ODR requires that certain entities have a single definition across the program; violating it leads to undefined behavior.
Use gdb/lldb, compile with -g, run sanitizers (ASan, UBSan), add logging, and use core dumps for post-mortem analysis.

interview.q: What are C++11/14/17/20 features to learn next?
interview.a: Focus on move semantics, smart pointers, lambdas, std::thread, std::optional, std::filesystem, constexpr, and concepts in C++20.
Avoid platform-specific APIs, use standard library where possible, conditionally compile platform code, and test on target platforms.

interview.q: Best practices for C++ code quality?
interview.a: Enable warnings (-Wall -Wextra), use static analysis (clang-tidy), write unit tests, use RAII, prefer smart pointers, and keep functions small and testable.

cpp_syntax.title: Basic Syntax & Types
cpp_syntax.summary: Primitives, auto, type inference, references, pointers, nullptr, enums and initialization styles.
cpp_syntax.details: Understand core types, initialization forms, value categories, and how to use references and pointers safely. Learn modern patterns: uniform initialization, scoped enums, and prefer 'auto' when the type is obvious. Master const-correctness and when to use move vs copy.

examples.code: int x{10}; double d = 3.14; auto s = std::string("hi"); int &r = x; int *p = &x;

interview.q: What is the difference between int and auto?
interview.a: int is an explicit type; auto asks the compiler to deduce the type from the initializer.

interview.q: What is brace initialization and why prefer it?
interview.a: Brace initialization (T x{...}) prevents narrowing conversions and unifies initialization syntax.
{} forbids narrowing and prefers initializer_list; = allows implicit conversions.

interview.q: What is a reference and how is it different from a pointer?
interview.a: A reference is an alias that cannot be reseated and must be initialized; a pointer can be null and reseated.
nullptr is the null pointer literal of type std::nullptr_t, safer than 0 or NULL.

interview.q: When to use enum class over enum?
interview.a: enum class provides scoped, strongly typed enumerations, avoiding implicit conversions.
Using using Name = Type; defines an alias. typedef is older syntax.
Mark values that shouldn't change with const to enable safer code and help the compiler optimize.
constexpr marks functions/variables evaluable at compile-time when given constant expressions.

interview.q: What is volatile and when to use it?
interview.a: volatile prevents certain compiler optimizations for memory that may change externally; rarely used, mostly for hardware/embedded.
Lvalues have identities/addressable; rvalues are temporaries. Rvalue references enable move semantics.
Multiple functions with same name but different parameter lists; overload resolution picks the best match.
Function parameters can have defaults provided in the declaration, used when caller omits them.
They are C++ casts with different guarantees: const_cast removes const, static_cast for safe conversions, reinterpret_cast for low-level, dynamic_cast for polymorphic downcasts.
Namespaces group identifiers to avoid name collisions; use namespace qualifiers or using directives carefully.

interview.q: How to pass large objects to functions?
interview.a: Pass by const reference or by value with move semantics; avoid unnecessary copies.
auto&& is a universal/forwarding reference that forward-captures lvalues and rvalues appropriately.
Aggregates (structs with public members) can be initialized with brace lists without constructors.
R"(text)" allow strings with quotes and backslashes without escaping.

interview.q: How to write readable C++ code?
interview.a: Use clear naming, small functions, const correctness, prefer standard algorithms, and document invariants.

cpp_oop.title: Classes & OOP
cpp_oop.summary: Constructors, destructors, copy/move semantics, encapsulation, virtual functions and polymorphism.
cpp_oop.details: Design classes with clear ownership and lifetime rules. Prefer composition over inheritance, keep interfaces small, and handle resource management via RAII. Understand copy/move semantics to avoid unexpected copies and ensure exception safety.

examples.code:
struct Vec{ int x,y; Vec(int a,int b):x(a),y(b){} };
class Base{ public: virtual void f(){}; virtual ~Base(){} };

interview.a: Encapsulation hides implementation details behind an interface, typically via private members and public methods.
Constructors initialize objects; destructors clean up. They manage resources via RAII.

interview.q: What is the Rule of Three/Five/Zero?
interview.a: If a class manages resources, implement copy constructor, copy assignment, and destructor (Rule of Three); with move semantics, consider move ctor/assign (Rule of Five); prefer no manual resource management (Rule of Zero).
Polymorphism allows treating derived types via base pointers/references using virtual functions for dynamic dispatch.
If a base class is deleted through a base pointer, a virtual destructor ensures derived destructors run and resources are freed.
A pure virtual function (=0) declares an interface that derived classes must implement, making the base abstract.
Multiple inheritance can cause ambiguity and the diamond problem; virtual inheritance and careful design mitigate issues.
Object slicing occurs when a derived object is copied into a base object by value, losing derived parts; use pointers/references to avoid it.
Decide ownership model: default copy if trivially copyable, delete copy if exclusive ownership, implement move for transfer semantics.
pImpl hides implementation details by storing a pointer to an impl type, reducing header dependencies and improving build times.
Curiously Recurring Template Pattern lets base templates use derived class types for static polymorphism and code reuse.
dynamic_cast safely downcasts polymorphic pointers/references, returning nullptr on failure for pointers or throwing for references.
public, protected, private control visibility of members; private for encapsulation, protected for derived access.
Define abstract base classes with pure virtual functions and virtual destructor for interfaces.
Prefer composing objects with member objects rather than inheritance to reduce coupling and increase reuse.
Use RAII, strong exception safety in operations, and avoid resource leaks by handling allocations and throwing carefully.

interview.q: What is the difference between aggregation and composition?
interview.a: Aggregation denotes a 'has-a' relationship with independent lifetimes; composition implies ownership and tied lifetimes.

interview.q: How to hide implementation details in headers?
interview.a: Use forward declarations, pImpl, and move as much as possible to .cpp files to reduce compile-time dependencies.
Overriding redefines a virtual function in a derived class; overloading defines functions with the same name but different signatures.
Keep small, single-responsibility classes, prefer explicit constructors, delete unwanted operations, and provide clear invariants.

cpp_memory.title: Memory Management & Smart Pointers
cpp_memory.summary: new/delete, smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr), ownership models and avoiding leaks & dangling pointers.
cpp_memory.details: Memory safety is critical: prefer smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr) and RAII. Understand ownership models, how to break cycles with weak_ptr, and when to use custom allocators for performance-critical code.

examples.code:
auto up = std::make_unique<MyClass>(args);
auto sp = std::make_shared<MyClass>(args);

// weak_ptr example
struct Node{ std::shared_ptr<Node> next; std::weak_ptr<Node> prev; };

interview.a: Use unique_ptr for exclusive ownership of a resource; it is lightweight and non-shared.
Use shared_ptr when multiple owners need shared lifetime and you accept the overhead of reference counting.

interview.q: How to avoid cyclic references with shared_ptr?
interview.a: Use weak_ptr for back-references so cycles are broken and memory can be reclaimed.
Use raw pointers for non-owning observers or to interoperate with APIs; avoid raw owning pointers.
A memory leak occurs when allocated memory is not reclaimed; RAII and smart pointers help prevent leaks.
A dangling pointer points to memory that was freed; accessing it is undefined behavior.
Prefer smart pointers; when using new/delete manually ensure matching deletes and use RAII wrappers.
Placement new constructs an object in a pre-allocated buffer; destructor must be called explicitly.
Allocators abstract memory allocation strategy for containers; std::allocator is default.

interview.q: How to manage arrays of objects?
interview.a: Prefer std::vector; if using new[], use delete[] and prefer smart wrappers instead.
make_unique/make_shared are safer, exception-safe factory functions that avoid manual new and improve efficiency.
A type that can be moved but not copied, e.g., unique_ptr; useful for exclusive ownership.

interview.q: How to debug memory issues in C++?
interview.a: Use sanitizers (ASan/UBSan), valgrind, and run under a debugger or use logging and unit tests.
Ownership can be unique (single owner), shared (multiple owners), or none (observers); model dictates pointer type.
shared_ptr has atomic reference counting overhead; unique_ptr has minimal overhead compared to raw pointers.
Smart pointers can accept custom deleters to manage resources other than memory (files, sockets).
Use smart pointers to base class (std::unique_ptr<Base>) to manage dynamic polymorphic objects.

interview.q: What is RAII relation to memory management?
interview.a: RAII ensures resources including memory are released in destructors, preventing leaks even on exceptions.
Use pool allocators or container reuse; avoid many small allocations in performance-critical code.

interview.q: What is nullptr_t and why use nullptr?
interview.a: nullptr_t is the type of nullptr, clarifying null pointer intent and resolving overloads correctly.

cpp_templates.title: Templates & Generics
cpp_templates.summary: Function and class templates, template type deduction, SFINAE, concepts (C++20) and common patterns.
cpp_templates.details: Templates enable compile-time generic programming. Learn to write clean template interfaces, constrain templates with concepts (C++20), and use traits and SFINAE where appropriate. Be mindful of compile-time cost and diagnostic clarity.

examples.code:
template<typename T> T add(T a, T b){ return a+b; }
// usage: auto v = add(1,2);

interview.a: Templates allow generic programming by defining patterns that the compiler instantiates with concrete types.
Substitution Failure Is Not An Error: template instantiation failures during overload resolution are ignored, enabling conditional overloads.
You can specialize templates for specific types to provide custom behavior.
Variadic templates accept any number of template parameters (template<typename... Args>) enabling flexible APIs.
constexpr enables compile-time evaluation; used with templates for metaprogramming and constants.

interview.q: What are template argument deduction rules?
interview.a: Deduction deduces template parameters from function arguments; reference collapsing and top-level cv-qualifiers affect deduction.
Concepts are constraints on template parameters that improve diagnostics and express intent.
Use std::enable_if, std::conditional, and trait templates to detect or transform types.

interview.q: What is CRTP and where used?
interview.a: Curiously Recurring Template Pattern implements static polymorphism and mixins by having a template base take derived class.

interview.q: Difference between function template and function template instantiation?
interview.a: Function template is a pattern; instantiation is the concrete function generated by the compiler.

interview.q: What is duck typing in templates?
interview.a: Templates accept types that meet required operations (duck typing) without explicit interfaces.
Use templates constrained by concepts or SFINAE and rely on iterators and iterator_traits.
Templates are type-safe, scope-aware, and integrated with language features; macros are textual substitutions.
Compile-time computation using templates to generate types or values; useful for optimization and DSLs.
Simplify code, use explicit template arguments, and read compiler errors carefully; modern compilers provide improved diagnostics.
Partial specialization customizes templates for a subset of parameter patterns, commonly used for class templates.
You can instantiate templates explicitly to move instantiation to a specific translation unit to reduce compile times.

interview.q: How to avoid code bloat with templates?
interview.a: Limit instantiations, use type erasure when appropriate, and prefer non-template helpers for common code.
Perfect forwarding preserves value category using forwarding references (T&& with template) and std::forward.

interview.q: When to use templates vs polymorphism?
interview.a: Use templates for compile-time genericity and performance; use runtime polymorphism when types are determined at runtime.

cpp_stl.title: STL & Common Containers
cpp_stl.summary: Vectors, strings, maps, sets, algorithms, iterators and best practices for choosing containers and algorithms.
cpp_stl.details: STL is a powerful toolbox: learn container characteristics (complexity, iterator invalidation), common algorithms, and how to combine them with lambdas and ranges. Prefer algorithms over hand-written loops for clarity and correctness.

examples.code: std::vector<int> v{3,1,2}; std::sort(v.begin(), v.end()); for(auto &x: v) std::cout<<x<<" ";

// erase-remove idiom
v.erase(std::remove(v.begin(), v.end(), 1), v.end());

interview.q: When to use std::vector vs std::list?
interview.a: Vector is generally better due to cache locality; list useful for constant-time insert/remove at arbitrary positions with iterators.
Certain container operations (e.g., vector reallocation) invalidate iterators and references; rules vary by container.
Prefer algorithms to loops for clarity and potential optimization; use iterators and lambda predicates.
emplace_back constructs in-place without copying/moving when given constructor args; push_back copies or moves an existing object.

interview.q: When to use unordered_map vs map?
interview.a: unordered_map offers average O(1) lookup; map is ordered with O(log n) lookup and uses less memory in some cases.
std::span is a non-owning view over contiguous sequences, useful for passing arrays and subranges.
Provide operator< or pass a comparison lambda to std::sort.

interview.q: What are algorithms that modify containers in place?
interview.a: std::remove is a shifting algorithm; use erase-remove idiom to actually remove elements from containers.
stable_sort preserves relative order of equivalent elements while sorting; may be slower but stable.
Use range-based for, iterators, or algorithms; avoid repeated container.size() checks in loops where possible.
An iterator adapter that moves elements when dereferenced, useful for move semantics in algorithms.

interview.q: How to choose container for concurrency?
interview.a: Choose containers and synchronization strategies carefully; many STL containers are not thread-safe for concurrent writes.

interview.q: What are adaptors like std::stack and std::queue?
interview.a: Container adaptors provide specific interfaces built on underlying containers (deque, vector, list).

interview.q: How to reserve capacity for performance?
interview.a: Call reserve for vectors/strings to avoid reallocations when size known in advance.
Iterator categories define capabilities: input, output, forward, bidirectional, and random-access iterators.
std::string_view is a non-owning view of a string, efficient for read-only operations without copying.
Use move semantics, references, or pass by const reference; use emplace and in-place construction.
Use container.erase(std::remove(container.begin(), container.end(), value), container.end()) to remove elements.
Standard algorithms accept execution policies (std::execution) enabling parallel execution where supported.
Measure hotspots, reduce allocations, batch operations, prefer contiguous containers, and consider custom allocators.

cpp_concurrency.title: Concurrency & Threading
cpp_concurrency.summary: std::thread, mutexes, atomics, futures, and high-level concurrency patterns; data races and synchronization primitives.
cpp_concurrency.details: Concurrency in C++ requires care: understand threading primitives, memory models, atomics, and synchronization. Prefer higher-level abstractions (thread pools, async) and test with sanitizers like TSan.

interview.q: What is a thread in C++?
interview.a: A thread represents a sequence of executing instructions; std::thread creates and manages threads.
A data race occurs when two threads access same memory concurrently and at least one is a write without synchronization.
Use mutexes, locks, atomics, or design lock-free algorithms to synchronize access.
std::mutex provides mutual exclusion; std::lock_guard manages lock lifetime to prevent leaks.
std::atomic types provide atomic operations without locks, useful for simple synchronization and lock-free patterns.

interview.q: What is deadlock and how to prevent it?
interview.a: Deadlock is circular waiting for locks; prevent by lock ordering, lock hierarchies, or using std::lock.
std::promise provides a value to a std::future which retrieves it, enabling communication between threads.
std::async runs a task asynchronously and returns a std::future for the result; policy controls launch behavior.
std::condition_variable allows threads to wait for events or conditions with notifications from other threads.
Lock-free code uses atomic operations to avoid mutexes, improving performance but harder to reason about.
Synchronize mutable shared state, minimize locking scope, document thread-safety guarantees, and use immutable data when possible.

interview.q: What is memory ordering in atomics?
interview.a: Memory orders (relaxed, acquire, release, seq_cst) define visibility and ordering guarantees for atomic operations.
Use shared_mutex to allow concurrent read access and exclusive write access for readers-writer patterns.
A race condition occurs when behavior depends on unpredictable scheduling, typically due to unsynchronized access.
Use thread sanitizer (TSan), add logging, reproduce with stress tests, and analyze synchronization logic.

interview.q: What are thread pools and why use them?
interview.a: Thread pools reuse a fixed set of threads to execute tasks, reducing overhead of thread creation.
False sharing is when unrelated variables on same cache line are modified by different threads, causing performance degradation.

interview.q: How to stop a thread safely?
interview.a: Use cooperative cancellation with atomic flags, std::stop_token (C++20), or joinable signaling rather than forceful termination.
A pipeline composes asynchronous tasks returning futures, chaining results and enabling concurrency with clearer flow.
Minimize shared mutable state, prefer immutable data, keep critical sections small, and profile for contention.

cpp_build.title: Build Systems & Tooling
cpp_build.summary: Compiler flags, linkers, CMake basics, debugging, sanitizers, and unit testing frameworks.
cpp_build.details: Understand compiler flags, how to structure projects with CMake, and use sanitizers and static analysis to catch bugs early. Learn LTO, ccache, and CI integration to scale development.

examples.code:
g++ -std=c++17 -Wall -Wextra -O2 main.cpp -o app
cmake -S . -B build && cmake --build build

// CMake snippet
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.12)
project(Example)
add_executable(example main.cpp)

interview.q: What is CMake and why use it?
interview.a: CMake is a cross-platform build system generator that produces native build files (Makefiles, Visual Studio, etc.), simplifying multi-platform builds.

interview.q: What are typical compiler warning flags?
interview.a: Use -Wall -Wextra and consider -Werror to treat warnings as errors; add targeted warnings for stricter checks.
Compile with -fsanitize=address, -fsanitize=undefined and link accordingly; use -g for better diagnostics.
Incremental build recompiles only changed sources, speeded by correct dependency tracking and out-of-source builds.

interview.q: How to speed up C++ builds?
interview.a: Use precompiled headers, unity builds, ccache, and reduce header dependencies via pImpl and forward declarations.
Link Time Optimization performs optimizations at link stage across translation units for better performance.
Use CMake build types or multi-config generators and set flags for optimization, debug info, and sanitizers per config.

interview.q: What is symbol visibility and why control it?
interview.a: Control exported symbols to reduce binary size and minimize ODR issues; use visibility attributes or export macros.
Stampless builds, fixed timestamps, consistent build environment, and deterministic tool versions improve reproducibility.

interview.q: What are static vs dynamic libraries?
interview.a: Static libraries (.a/.lib) are linked into executables; dynamic libraries (.so/.dll) are linked at runtime enabling smaller exes and shared updates.
Check undefined references, ensure correct library order, verify symbol mangling and linkage (extern "C"), and inspect object files.

interview.q: What is continuous integration (CI) for C++?
interview.a: CI automates builds, tests, and analysis across platforms on changes to detect regressions early.
Use frameworks like GoogleTest, Catch2, and run tests in CI with sanitizers and coverage reports.
Code coverage measures which code paths tests exercise, guiding additional test creation.
Tools like clang-tidy and cppcheck catch style issues, portability, and potential bugs; integrate into CI.

interview.q: Why build in debug with optimizations sometimes?
interview.a: Sometimes optimized debug builds (-O1 -g) reproduce bugs that only appear under optimization.

interview.q: What is a build matrix in CI?
interview.a: A build matrix runs combinations of compilers, OS, and configurations to ensure broad compatibility.
Strip symbols, reduce inlining where unnecessary, use LTO, and remove unused code and RTTI if safe.
Cross-compilation builds binaries for a different target platform than the host; set appropriate toolchains and sysroots.
Keep scripts idempotent, avoid environment assumptions, document dependencies, and automate checks in CI.

cpp_lambda.title: Lambdas & Functional Utilities
cpp_lambda.summary: Lambda syntax, capture modes, std::function, and using functional style with algorithms and ranges.
cpp_lambda.details: Lambdas are concise function objects; learn capture-by-value/reference, mutable, generic lambdas, and when to use std::function vs templated callables. Use lambdas to simplify algorithms and callbacks.

examples.code:
auto sum = [](int a,int b){ return a+b; };
std::cout << sum(2,3); // 5

interview.a: A lambda is an anonymous function object with optional captures, parameters, and body, declared with [](...) {...}.
[=] captures local variables by value, [&] captures by reference; can also capture specific variables.
mutable allows modifying captured-by-value variables inside the lambda body.
Generic lambdas use auto parameter types allowing templates-like behavior for parameter deduction.
Use std::function to store type-erased callables, but it has overhead compared to templates or direct callables.

interview.q: How to avoid allocations with lambdas in containers?
interview.a: Avoid std::function if possible; use templates or inline lambdas with algorithms to prevent allocations.
Use [v = std::move(x)] to move a variable into the lambda capture in C++14/C++17 style.

interview.q: How to pass lambdas to threads?
interview.a: std::thread accepts callables and captures copy/move semantics apply; be careful with references and lifetimes.
A closure is the function object created by a lambda that holds captured state.
C++17/20 allow certain lambdas to be constexpr if their body and captures are constexpr-compatible.
Assign named variables or breakpoints in the lambda, and ensure captured objects are alive when invoked.

interview.q: How do lambdas interact with templates?
interview.a: Lambdas are convertible to function objects; template functions can accept generic callables and deduce types.

interview.q: What is the closure type name?
interview.a: Closure types are unique compiler-generated types; use auto or std::function if you need to store them.

interview.q: When to use lambda vs named function?
interview.a: Use lambda for short local behavior and when capturing local state; use named functions for reuse and clarity.
Yes, using std::function or passing a self-reference via parameters or using Y-combinator patterns.

interview.q: What is capturing this in lambdas?
interview.a: Capture this by value [=] or explicitly [this]; in C++17 you can capture *this to copy the object.

interview.q: How to store type-erased callable cheaply?
interview.a: Use small-buffer optimization via small function wrappers or templates instead of std::function when performance-critical.

interview.q: What are side effects in lambdas?
interview.a: Lambdas may have side effects; be mindful when passing to algorithms that assume pure functions.

interview.q: How to use lambdas with algorithms?
interview.a: Pass lambdas as predicates or transformers to std::sort, std::transform, std::for_each, etc., for concise code.
Keep lambdas short, avoid heavy captures, prefer explicit captures, and document intent for readability.

cpp_rvalue.title: Move Semantics & Rvalue References
cpp_rvalue.summary: Move constructors/assignments, std::move, std::forward, avoiding unnecessary copies and enabling efficient resource transfers.
cpp_rvalue.details: Move semantics enable efficient transfers of resources. Learn to implement move constructors/assignments, mark them noexcept where possible, and prefer move-friendly APIs (std::move, emplace). Understand moved-from states and document them.

examples.code:
std::string s = "hello";
std::string t = std::move(s); // s is now moved-from (valid but unspecified)
struct Movable{ std::vector<int> v; Movable(Movable&&)=default; Movable& operator=(Movable&&)=default; };

interview.a: Move semantics transfer resources from one object to another without copying, enabled by rvalue references and move constructors/assignments.
std::move performs a cast to an rvalue reference, enabling move constructors or move assignments to be selected.
std::forward preserves the value category of a forwarding reference for perfect forwarding in templates.
Implement move constructor when your class owns resources to allow efficient transfer instead of expensive copies.
An rvalue reference (T&&) binds to temporaries and allows moving resources from them.
A forwarding reference (template T&&) binds to both lvalues and rvalues and forwards them appropriately.
Delete copy constructor/assignment or make them private; prefer move-only semantics when appropriate.
Move assignment transfers resources from the source and leaves it in a valid but unspecified state.

interview.q: What is noexcept in move operations?
interview.a: Declaring move operations noexcept often enables stronger optimizations and usage in containers like vector.
Use swap with a moved temporary to implement assignment that is strongly exception-safe.

interview.q: What is copy elision and RVO?
interview.a: Copy elision (including RVO) optimizes away temporary copies; compilers can construct return values in place.

interview.q: When to pass by value vs const ref?
interview.a: Passing by value and moving can be efficient for types with cheap moves; otherwise use const reference.

interview.q: How to write perfect forwarding constructors?
interview.a: Use template<typename U> X(U&& u) : member(std::forward<U>(u)) {} but beware of overloads/clashes.

interview.q: What is a moved-from object state?
interview.a: A moved-from object must be valid and destructible but may be empty or unspecified; document invariants.

interview.q: How do containers use move semantics?
interview.a: Containers move elements during reallocation or when using std::move iterators to avoid copies.
Profile and use tools; add logging or delete copy operations to force move usage in tests.
Returns std::move if move is noexcept, else returns lvalue to prefer copy when moves may throw.

interview.q: How to implement move for classes with pointers?
interview.a: Transfer pointer ownership and set source pointer to nullptr to avoid double delete.

interview.q: What pitfalls exist with move semantics?
interview.a: Using moved-from objects incorrectly, forgetting noexcept, or unintentionally selecting copies instead of moves.
Provide noexcept move where possible, prefer default move when safe, and document moved-from state.

cpp_modern.title: Modern C++ Features (C++11..C++20)
cpp_modern.summary: Structured bindings, constexpr, auto, range-based for, smart pointers, and C++17/20 enhancements (std::optional, concepts, ranges).
cpp_modern.details: Modern C++ adds expressive features that improve correctness and performance. Study structured bindings, constexpr, std::optional/variant, ranges, and concepts. Incrementally adopt features and ensure toolchain compatibility.

examples.code: if(auto opt = std::optional<int>{42}; opt){ auto v = *opt; std::cout<<v; }

// structured binding
std::pair<int,int> p{1,2}; auto [a,b] = p; // a==1, b==2

interview.a: Structured bindings unpack tuple-like objects into individual names: auto [a,b] = pair;
std::optional represents an optional value that may or may not be present, safer than sentinel values.
std::variant is a type-safe union to hold one of several types with visitation for access.
constexpr functions can be evaluated at compile time for constant expressions, enabling compile-time computation.
Inline variables allow definitions in headers without violating ODR, useful for constants.
Ranges provide composable views and actions for sequences, improving algorithm expressiveness.
Concepts constrain template parameters to express requirements, improving readability and diagnostics.
Coroutines enable suspendable functions for asynchronous programming and generators with language support.
std::filesystem provides portable APIs for file and path operations standardized in C++17.
std::any holds single values of any type with type-safe extraction; good for heterogeneous containers.

interview.q: What is a selection of modern idioms?
interview.a: Prefer auto, smart pointers, range-based loops, algorithms, and avoid raw loops for clarity and safety.
consteval forces evaluation at compile time; introduced to enforce compile-time computation.
Fold expressions provide concise syntax for variadic template parameter pack operations.
std::span is a non-owning view of contiguous memory useful for passing array-like data.
C++ modules (C++20) provide a modern alternative to headers that improve build times and encapsulation.
C++20 adds designated initializers for aggregate types, improving clarity of initialization.
std::chrono expanded with calendars, time zones, and formatting utilities in modern C++.

interview.q: How to use consteval vs constexpr?
interview.a: consteval forces compile-time execution; constexpr allows compile-time or runtime depending on usage.
std::jthread (C++20) automatically joins on destruction and supports cooperative cancellation with stop tokens.

interview.q: How to adopt modern features safely?
interview.a: Introduce features incrementally, write tests, and ensure toolchain supports the desired standard level.

cpp_testing.title: Testing & Quality
cpp_testing.summary: Unit testing frameworks (GoogleTest, Catch2), CI integration, code coverage, static analysis and sanitizers.
cpp_testing.details: Build a testing culture: unit tests, integration tests, fuzzing, sanitizers and static analysis. Integrate these checks into CI and run them on every change to catch regressions early.

examples.code:
// Run with ASan
// g++ -fsanitize=address -g test.cpp -o test && ./test

interview.q: Why write unit tests for C++?
interview.a: Unit tests catch regressions, verify behavior, and document expected functionality, making maintenance easier.
Add GoogleTest as a dependency, write TEST cases, and integrate into CMake to run tests during CI.
Sanitizers (ASan, UBSan, MSan) detect memory errors, undefined behavior, and uninitialized memory at runtime.
Compile with -fsanitize=address -g and run tests to detect heap/stack buffer overflows and use-after-free.
TDD writes tests before code to drive design and ensure correctness from the start.
Use tools like gcov, lcov, or other coverage tools to measure which parts of code your tests exercise.
Fuzzing supplies malformed or random input to find crashes, security bugs, and undefined behavior.

interview.q: How to run tests in CI?
interview.a: Configure CI pipelines to build, run tests, and fail on test failures; include sanitizers and coverage reporting.
Mocks replace dependencies in tests to control behavior and isolate units; frameworks like GoogleMock support mocking.
Use stress tests, thread sanitizers, deterministic scheduling tools, and careful synchronization in tests.
Regression tests ensure new changes do not reintroduce old bugs by running a test suite on changes.
Static analyzers find potential bugs and style issues early without executing code.
Benchmark critical paths with profiling tools and microbenchmarks; run performance tests in controlled environments.

interview.q: What is unit vs integration testing?
interview.a: Unit tests focus on small components in isolation; integration tests check interactions between components.
Investigate root causes, add retries sparingly, and make tests deterministic by controlling environment and timing.
Property-based testing generates inputs to validate general properties of code rather than specific examples.
Keep tests small, deterministic, expressive, and avoid tight coupling to implementation details.
Acceptance tests validate the system against user requirements and scenarios, often end-to-end.

interview.q: How to mock hardware or OS resources?
interview.a: Abstract dependencies, use interfaces, and provide test doubles or simulators to avoid coupling to real hardware.

interview.q: Best practices for C++ testing in CI?
interview.a: Run unit tests, integration tests, sanitizers, static analysis, and measure coverage in automated pipelines.

cpp_networking.title: Networking & Sockets
cpp_networking.summary: Basics of sockets, Boost.Asio for async networking, protocols (TCP/UDP) and designing networked applications.
cpp_networking.details: Networking requires solid understanding of sockets, non-blocking IO, and async patterns. Use well-tested libraries like Boost.Asio for portability and correctness. Pay attention to error handling, backpressure, and security (TLS).

examples.code:
// simple connect (blocking)
// int sock = socket(...); connect(sock, ...);

// Boost.Asio pseudo
// io_context ioc; tcp::acceptor acc(ioc, tcp::endpoint(tcp::v4(), 8080)); acc.async_accept(...);

interview.a: A socket is an endpoint for network communication used with protocols like TCP and UDP.
TCP is connection-oriented and reliable; UDP is connectionless and lightweight without guaranteed delivery.

interview.q: What is blocking vs non-blocking IO?
interview.a: Blocking IO waits for operations to complete; non-blocking IO returns immediately and uses polling or events.
Boost.Asio is a cross-platform C++ library for asynchronous IO, timers, and sockets with both sync and async APIs.
They are OS-level mechanisms to monitor multiple file descriptors for IO readiness, with epoll optimized for many descriptors.

interview.q: How to design a scalable server?
interview.a: Use asynchronous IO, thread pools, avoid per-connection threads, and manage resources to handle many connections.
Nagle's algorithm batches small TCP packets to reduce overhead; it can be disabled (TCP_NODELAY) for low-latency apps.

interview.q: What is endianness and how to handle it?
interview.a: Endianness is byte order; use network byte order (htonl/ntohl) for portable network protocols.

interview.q: How to use TLS in C++?
interview.a: Use libraries like OpenSSL, Botan, or libraries with TLS support and manage certificates and secure contexts.
Reuse connections to backends to reduce overhead of repeated connect/disconnect operations.
Backpressure controls the rate of data production to avoid overwhelming consumers or network buffers.
Use unit tests with mocks, integration tests with test servers, and simulate network faults and latency.
TCP keep-alive detects dead peers and maintains connections; HTTP Keep-Alive reuses TCP connections for multiple requests.
Loop until the requested amount is transferred, handling EAGAIN/EWOULDBLOCK for non-blocking sockets.
WebSocket provides bidirectional, persistent connections; HTTP is request-response stateless by default.

interview.q: How to do asynchronous callbacks safely?
interview.a: Use strand or synchronization primitives to serialize handlers and manage object lifetimes carefully.
Techniques that avoid copying data between kernel and user space, improving throughput (e.g., sendfile).
Validate input, use TLS, apply authentication/authorization, avoid buffer overflows, and handle errors robustly.
Mechanisms to locate services dynamically (DNS, Consul) used in distributed systems.

interview.q: Best practices for network error handling?
interview.a: Retry with backoff, log errors, detect partial failures, and design idempotent operations where possible.

cpp_patterns.title: Design Patterns & Idioms
cpp_patterns.summary: Common patterns (Singleton, Factory, Observer) and C++ idioms (pImpl, CRTP) adapted to modern C++ practices.
cpp_patterns.details: Learn practical patterns and modern idioms: favor composition and policy-based designs, use pImpl for ABI stability, and apply CRTP for static polymorphism when needed. Understand trade-offs and prefer simple, testable solutions.

examples.code:
// pImpl sketch
class Widget{ struct Impl; std::unique_ptr<Impl> p; public: Widget(); ~Widget(); };

interview.a: Factory encapsulates object creation, returning base-class pointers to concrete types, decoupling clients from concrete implementations.

interview.q: What is the Singleton pattern and its caveats?
interview.a: Singleton ensures a single instance, but global state can hinder testability and increase coupling; prefer dependency injection.
Observer allows objects to subscribe to events from a subject and be notified on changes, enabling decoupled communication.
pImpl hides implementation details in a separate implementation class pointed to by the interface, reducing header dependencies.
CRTP uses a template base class parameterized by the derived class to enable static polymorphism and compile-time customization.

interview.q: When to prefer strategy over inheritance?
interview.a: Use strategy (composition of behavior) to switch algorithms at runtime rather than deep inheritance hierarchies.

interview.q: What is RAII and how does it relate to patterns?
interview.a: RAII is the idiom of acquiring resources in constructors and releasing in destructors; many patterns rely on RAII for resource safety.

interview.q: What is type erasure and where to use it?
interview.a: Type erasure hides concrete types behind a uniform interface (e.g., std::function) enabling heterogeneous containers.
Adapter converts one interface to another, allowing incompatible interfaces to work together.
Decorator adds behavior to objects dynamically by wrapping them with additional functionality.
Proxy controls access to another object, adding lazy initialization, caching, or access control.
Command encapsulates actions as objects, enabling undo/redo, queuing, and decoupled invocation.

interview.q: How to implement Observer safely in C++?
interview.a: Use weak_ptr to avoid dangling observers, or careful lifetime management and unsubscribe mechanics.

interview.q: What is Visitor pattern and its trade-offs?
interview.a: Visitor separates operations from object structure; it is extensible for operations but less so for new element types.
Use templated policies for customizable behavior at compile-time enabling highly reusable libraries.
Base choice on coupling, extensibility, performance, and testability; prefer simple solutions first.
Iterator provides a way to access elements of a collection sequentially without exposing underlying representation.
Mediator centralizes complex communications between objects, reducing direct coupling.

interview.q: How to use design patterns with modern C++?
interview.a: Leverage RAII, smart pointers, templates, and standard library utilities to implement patterns more safely and concisely.

interview.q: Best practices applying patterns in C++?
interview.a: Understand trade-offs, prefer composition, avoid premature abstraction, and write tests to validate pattern choices.
